---
title: "Chapter 31 — 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 30 · 📚 Large Language Models](ch30.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [32 · 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search →](ch32.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

A pretrained large language model knows an enormous amount but obeys no one in particular: it predicts plausible continuations, not helpful answers. This chapter is about **steering** — the spectrum of techniques that turn a raw foundation model into something that does what you want, ordered from cheapest and most reversible (writing a better prompt) to most invasive and permanent (changing the weights). Knowing where on that spectrum a problem belongs is one of the most valuable practical skills in applied AI, because each rung up the ladder costs more and buys a different kind of control.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** the "last mile" of applied LLMs (Part: Language, Vision & Modalities) · used to specialize a generic foundation model into a reliable, on-task, well-behaved assistant · the one key idea: steer at the cheapest layer that works — context, then weights, then preferences.
:::

## 31.1 — The steering ladder: context, knowledge, weights

Before reaching for any technique, get the mental model straight. A deployed LLM behaves as a function of three things you can change: the **context** you feed it (the prompt), the **knowledge** it can reach at inference (retrieval), and the **weights** themselves (fine-tuning). Steering means choosing which of these to touch — and the discipline is to touch the smallest one that solves the problem.

Think of it like fixing a confused new employee. The cheapest fix is to give clearer instructions (the prompt): it changes nothing permanent, costs one API call to test, and is fully reversible — but it only works within what the model already knows and can fit in its context window. The next fix is to hand them the relevant binder to read while they work — this is **retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)**: instead of teaching the model new facts, you fetch relevant documents at query time and paste them into the context. The most expensive fix is to send them back to training — **fine-tuning** — where you actually update the weights with gradient descent. That can change behavior the prompt can't reach (tone, format adherence, a skill the base model lacks) but it costs training compute, a curated dataset, and the risk of breaking things that already worked.

A one-line rule captures the whole chapter: prompting changes *what you say to* the model, RAG changes *what the model can see*, fine-tuning changes *what the model is*. Most production systems use all three in layers, and the later sections fill in each rung.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    P["Prompt engineering<br/>(context, reversible, cheap)"] --> R["RAG<br/>(inject knowledge at query time)"]
    R --> F["Fine-tuning<br/>(change weights, permanent)"]
    F --> A["Alignment<br/>(shape preferences & behavior)"]
    style P fill:#1f7a3d,color:#fff
    style A fill:#7a1f1f,color:#fff
```

## 31.2 — Prompting and in-context learning

The surprising empirical fact behind all prompting is **in-context learning**: a large enough model can pick up a task from examples placed in its prompt, with *no weight updates at all*. The forward pass alone does the adaptation — the examples condition the model's prediction the same way the opening of a sentence conditions its ending. Nobody fully understands why this ability emerges with scale, but it is the foundation of everything in this section.

The basic dial is how many examples you provide. **Zero-shot** gives only an instruction ("Classify the sentiment of this review:"). **Few-shot** prepends a handful of solved examples (the *shots*) so the model infers both the pattern and the expected output format:

```text
Review: "Loved it, would buy again."   Sentiment: positive
Review: "Broke on day two."            Sentiment: negative
Review: "It's fine, nothing special."  Sentiment:        <- model completes
```

Few-shot helps most when the *format* is unusual or the task is ambiguous; for tasks the model already understands, a crisp zero-shot instruction often matches it with far fewer tokens. The examples are not free — they consume context budget on every call — which is one reason Section 31.4 treats "few-shot examples eat the context" as a signal to fine-tune.

For multi-step reasoning, the single highest-leverage trick is **chain-of-thought (CoT)**: instruct the model to "think step by step" and write out intermediate reasoning before the final answer. The intuition is that each generated token is a fixed slice of computation, so forcing the model to *show its work* gives it more forward-pass steps to spend before committing to an answer — and arithmetic, logic, and word problems improve sharply. The cost is latency and tokens, and a crucial caveat: the reasoning is a *plausible-looking* trace, not a guaranteed-faithful account of the computation that produced the answer. Never treat a CoT trace as an audit log of what the model "really" did.

**Self-consistency** stacks on top of CoT. Instead of trusting one chain, you sample several independent reasoning paths (with a non-zero temperature so they actually differ) and take a majority vote over the final answers. The idea is that a correct answer can be reached by many different valid routes, while mistakes tend to be idiosyncratic — so answers that several independent chains agree on are more trustworthy than any single chain.

```python
# self-consistency: majority vote over k sampled chains
from collections import Counter

def self_consistency(llm, prompt, k=5, temp=0.7):
    answers = [extract_final(llm(prompt, temperature=temp)) for _ in range(k)]
    return Counter(answers).most_common(1)[0][0]  # modal answer wins
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Put the instruction *and* the single most constraining requirement at the very end of the prompt. Models attend strongly to the final tokens, so "Answer with a single word." lands harder as the last line than buried in a preamble.
:::

## 31.3 — Structured output and decoding controls

An LLM that returns free-form prose is useless as a software component if the next step in your pipeline is `json.loads()`. **Structured output** is the practice of forcing responses into a machine-parseable shape — typically JSON matching a schema. The weak way is to ask nicely ("Respond only in JSON"); the model usually complies but occasionally prepends an apology or wraps the JSON in chatter, and that occasional failure breaks production. The strong way is **constrained decoding**, where the serving stack masks the next-token probabilities at each step so that only tokens which keep the output valid against a grammar or JSON schema are even allowed. With true constrained decoding the output is parseable *by construction* — it physically cannot emit a stray token before the opening brace.

Underneath all of this sits the **decoding** step that turns the model's probability distribution into actual tokens, and a few knobs govern it. At each position the model emits a vector of **logits** (raw scores, one per vocabulary token); **temperature** $T$ rescales them before the softmax:

$$p_i = \frac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j / T)}$$

As $T \to 0$ the distribution collapses onto its single most likely token (greedy, deterministic); as $T$ rises it flattens, making rarer tokens more likely and the output more diverse. Temperature alone, though, can still occasionally sample a genuinely bad token from the long tail, so two **truncation** knobs restrict *which* tokens are eligible before sampling:

| Control | What it does | Turn it up for | Turn it down for |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Scales logit sharpness | Creative writing, brainstorming | Extraction, code, classification |
| Top-$k$ | Keep only the $k$ likeliest tokens | More variety | Tighter, safer output |
| Top-$p$ (nucleus) | Keep the smallest set whose cumulative mass $\geq p$ | More variety | Determinism |

The practical recipe is boring: for anything that has a *right answer* — extraction, classification, code — push temperature toward 0 and lean on top-$p$/top-$k$ to clip the tail. Reserve high temperature for tasks where variety *is* the goal.

::: {.callout-warning}
"Temperature 0" is not a guarantee of identical outputs across runs. Batching, GPU non-determinism, and floating-point summation order can still flip a token. For tasks that demand reproducibility, fix a seed where the API exposes one — and still expect occasional drift.
:::

## 31.4 — The prompt vs RAG vs fine-tune decision

This is the decision people get wrong most often, almost always by reaching for fine-tuning when a prompt would have done the job. The way to get it right is to first diagnose what is *actually* missing, rather than jumping to the most powerful tool.

The clean separation is this: prompting fixes a **behavior** the model can already do but isn't doing; RAG fixes missing, private, or changing **knowledge**; fine-tuning fixes a **skill or style** the model lacks even when given perfect instructions. The classic, expensive mistake is trying to fine-tune *facts* into a model. It is slow and costly, the facts go stale the moment the world changes, and worst of all the model tends to absorb the *style* of your answers rather than reliably memorizing their content — so it will confidently produce wrong-but-similar facts. Volatile or proprietary facts almost always belong in RAG, where you can update the source documents without retraining anything.

| Symptom | Best lever | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Wrong format / tone / ignores instructions | Prompt first, then fine-tune | Behavior is reachable in-context |
| Needs current, private, or large factual corpus | RAG | Facts change; weights shouldn't memorize them |
| Must master a niche format or style at scale | Fine-tune | Beyond what instructions reliably convey |
| Few-shot works but examples eat the context budget | Fine-tune | Bake the pattern into weights, free up tokens |
| Needs both fresh facts *and* a learned skill | RAG + fine-tune | Orthogonal problems, combine the levers |

::: {.callout-tip}
Always exhaust prompting before fine-tuning. The order of attempts should be: zero-shot → few-shot → RAG → fine-tune. Each step up costs more and is harder to undo, and a good prompt is frequently within a few points of a fine-tune at a fraction of the effort and zero maintenance burden.
:::

## 31.5 — Fine-tuning: full vs parameter-efficient, and the LoRA update

When you do decide to touch the weights, the brute-force version is **full fine-tuning**: continue training every parameter on your dataset. It is maximally expressive but brutally expensive in memory — for a 70B-parameter model you must hold not just the weights but their gradients and the optimizer state (with Adam, roughly $3$–$4\times$ the weight memory), and you walk away with a full-size copy of the model *per task*. That does not scale when you want ten specialized variants.

**Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT)** sidesteps this by *freezing* the pretrained weights and training only a tiny number of new parameters. The dominant method is **LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)**. The intuition rests on an empirical observation: the *update* that fine-tuning applies to a weight matrix is approximately **low-rank** — it lives in a far smaller subspace than the full matrix — so there is no need to spend the matrix's full degrees of freedom representing it. Instead, represent the update as the product of two skinny matrices.

For a frozen weight matrix $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$, LoRA keeps $W_0$ fixed and learns a low-rank correction $\Delta W = BA$:

$$h = W_0 x + \Delta W x = W_0 x + \frac{\alpha}{r}\, B A x$$

where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$, and the **rank** $r \ll \min(d, k)$. Only $A$ and $B$ train. The clever initialization is that $A$ starts from a small random draw while $B$ starts at exactly zero, so $\Delta W = BA = 0$ at step one — training begins *precisely* at the pretrained model and only departs from it as the data demands. The scalar $\alpha/r$ is a fixed knob controlling how loudly the adapter speaks relative to the frozen base. The trainable parameter count drops from $d{\times}k$ to $r(d+k)$ — for $d=k=4096$ and $r=8$ that is about $67\text{k}$ parameters instead of $\sim 17\text{M}$ per matrix, a roughly $250\times$ reduction.

The geometry is the whole point: a wide update is forced to pass through a narrow $r$-dimensional bottleneck.

<svg viewBox="0 0 420 140" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11">
  <rect x="10" y="20" width="80" height="100" fill="#1f7a3d" opacity="0.85"/>
  <text x="50" y="135" text-anchor="middle">B (d×r)</text>
  <text x="50" y="74" text-anchor="middle" fill="#fff">tall</text>
  <rect x="110" y="60" width="100" height="20" fill="#7a1f1f" opacity="0.85"/>
  <text x="160" y="95" text-anchor="middle">A (r×k)</text>
  <text x="160" y="74" text-anchor="middle" fill="#fff">wide</text>
  <text x="232" y="74" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16">=</text>
  <rect x="255" y="20" width="100" height="100" fill="none" stroke="#888" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/>
  <text x="305" y="135" text-anchor="middle">ΔW (d×k)</text>
  <text x="305" y="74" text-anchor="middle" fill="#888">rank ≤ r</text>
</svg>

```python
import numpy as np
# LoRA forward for one linear layer
d, k, r = 4096, 4096, 8
W0 = np.random.randn(d, k) * 0.02     # frozen pretrained weight
A  = np.random.randn(r, k) * 0.01     # trainable, small random init
B  = np.zeros((d, r))                 # trainable, zero -> dW = 0 at start
alpha = 16

def forward(x):
    return W0 @ x + (alpha / r) * (B @ (A @ x))   # base + low-rank delta

x = np.random.randn(k)
assert np.allclose(forward(x), W0 @ x)  # adapter is a no-op until B trains
```

**QLoRA** pushes this further for memory-bound setups: it quantizes the frozen base weights down to 4-bit (so the giant frozen matrix barely occupies memory) and then trains the LoRA adapters in higher precision on top. Because the base is never updated, its quantization error is harmless — it just sits there as a fixed, compressed backdrop — and this is precisely what lets a single consumer GPU fine-tune a model that full fine-tuning could never even hold in memory. A further practical bonus of all PEFT methods: the adapters are tiny (megabytes), so you can keep dozens of task-specific adapters on disk and hot-swap them over one shared, loaded-once base model.

::: {.callout-warning}
Choosing the rank $r$ is a real tradeoff. Too large and you forfeit the efficiency and can overfit a small dataset; too small and you starve the adapter of capacity for a genuinely hard task. Start small ($r=8$ or $16$) and raise it only if the model is clearly underfitting. And note that LoRA adds *zero* inference cost once you **merge** the adapter — fold $BA$ back into $W_0$ before serving and the model is structurally identical to the original.
:::

## 31.6 — Instruction tuning and supervised fine-tuning (SFT)

A base model only knows how to *complete text*; ask it "What is the capital of France?" and it might happily continue with another trivia question instead of answering. **Instruction tuning** — a form of **supervised fine-tuning (SFT)** — fixes this by training the model on (instruction, ideal-response) pairs so it learns the *behavior* of being a helpful assistant that answers rather than merely continues. This is the single step that turns a raw text predictor into a chatbot, and it is the prerequisite for every alignment method in the next two sections.

Two mechanics make SFT work in practice. The first is the **chat template**: a multi-turn conversation has to be serialized into one flat token stream, and special tokens mark where each role's turn begins and ends so the model can tell system, user, and assistant apart. Every model family ships its own template, and using the wrong one — at training or at inference — is one of the most common causes of silently degraded behavior.

```text
<|system|>You are a terse assistant.<|end|>
<|user|>Capital of France?<|end|>
<|assistant|>Paris.<|end|>
```

The second mechanic is **loss masking**. During SFT you only want the model to learn to *generate the assistant's tokens* — you do not want it learning to predict the user's questions, which are inputs, not things the model should produce. So the loss is computed only over the assistant-response token positions; the prompt tokens are masked out by setting their label to the special "ignore" index (conventionally $-100$, which the cross-entropy implementation skips). The objective is the ordinary next-token cross-entropy, but averaged over the unmasked positions only:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{SFT}} = -\frac{1}{|\mathcal{R}|}\sum_{t \in \mathcal{R}} \log p_\theta\big(y_t \mid y_{<t}\big)$$

where $\mathcal{R}$ is the set of assistant-response token positions. Skip the masking and the model wastes capacity learning to predict user inputs — and can start parroting prompts back at inference time.

```python
# build the label mask: -100 everywhere except assistant tokens
IGNORE = -100
labels = [tok if is_assistant[t] else IGNORE
          for t, tok in enumerate(token_ids)]
# cross-entropy implementations skip positions whose label == -100
```

## 31.7 — Alignment with human feedback: RLHF

SFT teaches the model to imitate good answers, but it has a ceiling: for fuzzy qualities like helpfulness, harmlessness, and honesty, it is far easier for a human to *compare* two answers and say which is better than to *write* the single ideal answer from scratch. **Alignment** harnesses exactly that comparison signal to push the model toward the responses people actually prefer. The classic recipe is **RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)**, and it runs in three stages.

1. **SFT** — start from an instruction-tuned model (Section 31.6) so the policy already produces sane, on-format responses.
2. **Reward model (RM)** — collect human preference pairs of the form (prompt, response A, response B, "A is better"), and train a model $r_\phi$ that maps a (prompt, response) to a scalar score. It is trained so the preferred response scores higher, via the **Bradley–Terry** pairwise loss:
   $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{RM}} = -\log \sigma\big(r_\phi(x, y_w) - r_\phi(x, y_l)\big)$$
   where $y_w$ is the winning (preferred) response, $y_l$ the losing one, and $\sigma$ the logistic function. Intuitively this just pushes the score gap between winner and loser to be large and positive.
3. **RL optimization** — fine-tune the policy (the LLM itself) with **PPO** to maximize the reward model's score, while a **KL penalty** holds it close to the SFT model so it doesn't drift into degenerate text that games the RM:
   $$\max_\theta\; \mathbb{E}_{x,\,y\sim \pi_\theta}\big[\,r_\phi(x,y)\,\big] \;-\; \beta\, \mathrm{KL}\!\big(\pi_\theta(\cdot\mid x)\,\|\,\pi_{\text{ref}}(\cdot\mid x)\big)$$

The KL term, weighted by $\beta$, is the safety leash: maximize reward, but don't wander far from the reference policy. (PPO itself — the policy-gradient machinery that makes this optimization stable — is owned by the Deep RL chapter; here it is just the optimizer turning a scalar reward into weight updates.)

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    SFT["SFT model"] --> RM["Reward model<br/>(preference pairs)"]
    SFT --> PPO["PPO policy"]
    RM -->|reward r| PPO
    SFT -->|KL leash| PPO
    PPO --> OUT["Aligned model"]
```

## 31.8 — Direct preference optimization and the preference family

RLHF works, but operationally it is a beast: you train a *separate* reward model, run an unstable RL loop, and juggle up to four models in memory at once (policy, reference, reward, and a value head). **DPO (Direct Preference Optimization)** asks a sharp, lazy-in-the-best-sense question — if the reward model's only job is to *rank* responses, why train it and run RL at all? DPO's key result is that the RLHF objective has a *closed-form* optimal policy, which lets you algebraically eliminate the reward model and rewrite the whole problem as a single **classification loss directly on the preference pairs** — no reward model, no sampling loop, no RL.

The mechanism: the optimal RLHF policy implies that the reward is recoverable from the policy itself, $r(x,y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x)} + \text{const}$. Substituting this expression into the Bradley–Terry loss makes the reward model cancel out and leaves a loss you can minimize with ordinary supervised training:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}} = -\log \sigma\!\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_w\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w\mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_l\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l\mid x)}\right)$$

In words: raise the policy's probability on the preferred response $y_w$ and lower it on the rejected $y_l$ — but measure each *relative to* the frozen reference model, so the model can't simply inflate or deflate all probabilities to cheat. The reference in the denominator plays exactly the stabilizing role the KL penalty played in RLHF. DPO is simpler, more stable, and far cheaper to run, which is why it became the default open-source alignment method.

DPO opened a whole family of preference-optimization methods, each tweaking the loss to patch a known weakness:

| Method | Core idea / what it fixes |
|---|---|
| **DPO** | Closed-form RLHF rewritten as a classification loss on preference pairs |
| **IPO** | Adds regularization to curb DPO's tendency to overfit the preference margin |
| **KTO** | Learns from a *single* "good"/"bad" label per sample — no pairs needed, so cheaper data |
| **ORPO** | Folds the preference signal into SFT itself, dropping the reference model — one stage, not two |
| **SimPO** | Uses a length-normalized reward and no reference model — directly fights the verbosity bias |

::: {.callout-warning}
**Reward hacking** and **catastrophic forgetting** are the two failure modes to watch. Reward hacking is when the policy discovers responses the RM scores high but humans actually dislike — sycophancy, padding, or exploiting an RM blind spot. The classic example is **verbosity bias**, where longer answers get systematically over-rewarded (which is exactly what SimPO's length normalization targets). Catastrophic forgetting is when aligning or fine-tuning hard on a narrow objective erodes general capabilities the base model used to have. The standard defenses are the KL/reference leash, mixing general-purpose data back into the tuning set, and simply not over-training.
:::

## 31.x — Key takeaways

- Steer at the **cheapest layer that works**: prompt → RAG → fine-tune. Each rung costs more, buys a different kind of control, and is harder to reverse.
- **In-context learning** lets a model learn a task from prompt examples with no weight updates; zero/few-shot, chain-of-thought, and self-consistency are all variations on feeding the model the right context.
- **Decoding controls** — temperature, top-$k$, top-$p$ — trade determinism for diversity; **constrained decoding** makes structured/JSON output valid by construction rather than by polite request.
- The decision rule: prompting fixes **behavior**, RAG fixes **knowledge**, fine-tuning fixes **skill/style**. Do not fine-tune volatile facts — they belong in RAG.
- **LoRA** learns a low-rank update $\Delta W = \frac{\alpha}{r}BA$ on frozen weights (with $B$ initialized to zero, so training starts as a no-op); **QLoRA** adds a 4-bit frozen base so the whole thing fits on one GPU. Adapters merge back in at zero inference cost.
- **SFT / instruction tuning** relies on the right **chat template** and on **loss masking** (train only on assistant tokens) to turn a base completer into an assistant.
- **RLHF** = reward model (Bradley–Terry) + PPO + KL leash. **DPO** collapses all of that into one stable classification loss on preference pairs; IPO/KTO/ORPO/SimPO refine it.
- Watch for **reward hacking** (gaming the reward, e.g. verbosity bias) and **catastrophic forgetting** (losing general ability while specializing).

## 31.y — See also

- **Chapter 30 — Large Language Models** — the base models these techniques steer, and where pretraining ends and adaptation begins.
- **Chapter 32 — Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search** — the "inject knowledge at query time" lever in depth.
- **Chapter 33 — AI Agents & Tool Use** — prompting and structured output as the substrate for tool-calling agents.
- **Chapter 25 — Deep Reinforcement Learning** — PPO and the policy-gradient machinery that RLHF borrows.
- **Chapter 15 — Training Deep Neural Networks** — the optimization, regularization, and forgetting dynamics underlying all fine-tuning.
- **Chapter 46 — AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance** — why alignment matters beyond benchmark scores.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 30 · 📚 Large Language Models](ch30.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [32 · 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search →](ch32.html)
